README.html.title: Invex.FileSystem | Invex.FileSystem
README.html.summary: Invex.FileSystem A strongly-typed file system abstraction for .NET that layers build-aware path resolution and reversible file transformations on top of System.IO.Abstractions. Stop hard-coding paths and scattering magic strings across your build and tooling code. Invex.FileSystem lets you resolve well-known locations by key, compose paths fluently, and make temporary, automatically-rolled-back edits to files. Features Rooted paths RootedPath carries its own IFileSystem so paths and I/O always travel together. Compose them fluently with the / operator. Keyed path resolution resolve locations such as "Root" or "Artifacts" through a prioritized chain of IPathProvider implementations, with results cached after first lookup. Strongly-typed path markers give compile-time names to well-known paths via IPathMarker instead of relying on magic strings. Reversible file transforms TransformFileScope and TransformMultiFileScope apply temporary edits to one or many files and restore the originals on dispose (or commit them with CancelRestore). Dependency-injection first a single AddRootedFileSystem() call wires everything up; register custom providers with ProvidePath(...). Installation dotnet add package Invex.FileSystem Quick start Register the file system with your DI container and declare where your well-known paths live: using Invex.FileSystem; using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection; var services = new ServiceCollection(); services.AddLogging(); // Register the rooted file system. services.AddRootedFileSystem(); // Describe where your well-known paths live. services.ProvidePath((key, fs) => key switch { "Root" => fs.CurrentDirectory, "Artifacts" => fs.GetPath("Root") / "artifacts", _ => null, }); using var provider = services.BuildServiceProvider(); var fs = provider.GetRequiredService<IRootedFileSystem>(); // Resolve and compose paths. RootedPath artifacts = fs.GetPath("Artifacts"); RootedPath logFile = artifacts / "logs" / "build.log"; if (!logFile.PathExists) fs.Directory.CreateDirectory(artifacts); Core concepts RootedPath A RootedPath is a record that binds an absolute path string to the IFileSystem it belongs to. Because the file system travels with the path, existence checks and queries need no extra context: RootedPath path = fs.GetPath("Root") / "src" / "Project.csproj"; bool exists = path.PathExists; // file or directory bool isFile = path.FileExists; RootedPath? parent = path.Parent; // null at the root string nameNoExt = path.FileNameWithoutExtension; // Implicitly converts to string for APIs that expect one. File.ReadAllText(path); Use the / operator to combine segments and the implicit string conversion to pass a path to any standard API. Path providers Path resolution is decoupled from I/O. IRootedFileSystem.GetPath(string) queries every registered IPathProvider in * descending priority order* and returns the first non-null result, caching it for subsequent calls. Register providers inline with ProvidePath: // Simple key -> path mapping (priority defaults to 1). services.ProvidePath(key => key == "Temp" ? new RootedPath(new FileSystem(), Path.GetTempPath()) : null); // Provider that depends on another resolved path. services.ProvidePath((key, fs) => key == "Output" ? fs.GetPath("Root") / "output" : null, priority: 5); For more complex resolution logic, implement IPathProvider directly and register it as a service. Higher Priority values win when multiple providers can resolve the same key; use a negative priority for a low-priority fallback. Path markers When a path is statically determined by its type, implement IPathMarker to give it a strongly-typed name. Unlike keyed lookups, markers bypass the provider chain and cache: public sealed class ArtifactsPath : IPathMarker { public static RootedPath Path(IFileSystem fileSystem) => ((IRootedFileSystem)fileSystem).GetPath("Root") / "artifacts"; } RootedPath artifacts = fs.GetPath<ArtifactsPath>(); Reversible file transforms TransformFileScope captures a file's content, applies a transform, and restores the original when the scope is disposed — ideal for build steps that must temporarily patch a file (such as injecting a version number) without leaving permanent changes. RootedPath projectFile = fs.GetPath("Root") / "Directory.Build.props"; await using (var scope = await TransformFileScope.CreateAsync( projectFile, content => content.Replace("1.0.0", "2.0.0"))) { // The file now contains "2.0.0" — run your build step here. } // On dispose the original content is restored automatically. Chain additional edits fluently, and call CancelRestore() to keep the changes instead of rolling them back: await using var scope = await TransformFileScope .CreateAsync(projectFile, _ => "version=1") .AddAsync(content => content + "\nbuild=ci"); scope.CancelRestore(); // commit the transformation permanently If a file does not exist when the scope is created, it is created during the scope and deleted on restore. Use TransformMultiFileScope to patch a set of files in parallel with the same all-or-nothing restore guarantee: RootedPath[] projects = [ fs.GetPath("Root") / "src" / "A" / "A.csproj", fs.GetPath("Root") / "src" / "B" / "B.csproj", ]; await using var scope = await TransformMultiFileScope.CreateAsync( projects, content => content.Replace("<Version>1.0.0</Version>", "<Version>2.0.0</Version>")); Testing Because the library builds on System.IO.Abstractions, you can construct a RootedPath over a MockFileSystem and exercise your code without touching the real disk: var mock = new MockFileSystem(); var path = new RootedPath(mock, "/repo/file.txt"); mock.AddFile(path, new MockFileData("hello")); path.FileExists.ShouldBeTrue(); API reference Type Description IRootedFileSystem File system abstraction with GetPath, CurrentDirectory, and CreateRootedPath. RootedPath A path bound to an IFileSystem, with composition and existence helpers. IPathProvider Resolves a RootedPath for a key; queried by priority. FunctionPathProvider Delegate-based IPathProvider implementation. IPathMarker Strongly-typed, statically-computed path. TransformFileScope Temporary, reversible single-file transformation. TransformMultiFileScope Temporary, reversible multi-file transformation. FileSystemHostExtensions DI registration: AddRootedFileSystem and ProvidePath. License Licensed under the MIT License.

api/Invex.FileSystem.FunctionPathProvide.title: Class FunctionPathProvider | Invex.FileSystem
api/Invex.FileSystem.FunctionPathProvide.summary: Class FunctionPathProvider Namespace Invex.FileSystem Assembly Invex.FileSystem.dll A lightweight, delegate-based implementation of IPathProvider that resolves paths using a caller-supplied function rather than requiring a dedicated class. [PublicAPI] public sealed class FunctionPathProvider : IPathProvider Inheritance object FunctionPathProvider Implements IPathProvider Inherited Members object.Equals(object) object.Equals(object, object) object.GetHashCode() object.GetType() object.ReferenceEquals(object, object) object.ToString() Remarks Use FunctionPathProvider (or the ProvidePath extension in FileSystemHostExtensions) when path logic is simple enough to express inline rather than in a separate class. For complex resolution logic — such as searching the file system or combining multiple keys — a dedicated IPathProvider implementation is clearer. Properties Priority Gets the priority of this provider. public required int Priority { get; init; } Property Value int Remarks Higher values are queried before lower values. When two providers can both resolve the same key the one with the higher priority wins. Use a negative value to register a low-priority fallback that only applies when no other provider matches. Provider Gets the delegate that implements the path resolution logic. public required Func<string, RootedPath?> Provider { get; init; } Property Value Func<string, RootedPath> Remarks The delegate receives the key string and should return a RootedPath when it recognizes the key, or null to defer resolution to the next provider in the chain. Methods GetPath(string) Attempts to resolve a RootedPath for the given key. public RootedPath? GetPath(string key) Parameters key string The key identifying the path to locate (e.g. "Root", "Artifacts"). Returns RootedPath A RootedPath if this provider recognises key; otherwise null to pass resolution to the next provider in the chain.

api/Invex.FileSystem.IPathMarker.html.title: Interface IPathMarker | Invex.FileSystem
api/Invex.FileSystem.IPathMarker.html.summary: Interface IPathMarker Namespace Invex.FileSystem Assembly Invex.FileSystem.dll Defines a contract for types that represent a specific, well-known file path within the file system, resolved relative to the current System.IO.Abstractions.IFileSystem instance. [PublicAPI] public interface IPathMarker Remarks Implement this interface (typically as a nested type or a source-generated partial class) to give a strongly-typed name to a path that would otherwise be identified by a magic string. Unlike keys resolved through GetPath(string), IPathMarker paths are statically computed by the marker type itself via the Path(IFileSystem) static abstract method. Methods Path(IFileSystem) Computes the rooted path that this marker represents, evaluated against the given fileSystem. public static abstract RootedPath Path(IFileSystem fileSystem) Parameters fileSystem IFileSystem The file system instance to bind the path to. Passing the current IRootedFileSystem keeps the returned RootedPath consistent with all other paths resolved by the application. Returns RootedPath A RootedPath representing the location of the file or directory.

api/Invex.FileSystem.IPathProvider.html.title: Interface IPathProvider | Invex.FileSystem
api/Invex.FileSystem.IPathProvider.html.summary: Interface IPathProvider Namespace Invex.FileSystem Assembly Invex.FileSystem.dll Defines a provider that can resolve a RootedPath for a given key within the file system. [PublicAPI] public interface IPathProvider Remarks Multiple providers can be registered simultaneously. GetPath(string) queries them in descending Priority order and returns the first non-null result. A provider that does not recognize a key should return null to let the next provider in the chain handle it. Because GetPath(string) caches successful resolutions, a provider's GetPath(string) method is called at most once per key per lifetime of the IRootedFileSystem instance. Properties Priority Gets the priority of this provider. int Priority { get; } Property Value int Remarks Higher values are queried before lower values. When two providers can both resolve the same key the one with the higher priority wins. Use a negative value to register a low-priority fallback that only applies when no other provider matches. Methods GetPath(string) Attempts to resolve a RootedPath for the given key. RootedPath? GetPath(string key) Parameters key string The key identifying the path to locate (e.g. "Root", "Artifacts"). Returns RootedPath A RootedPath if this provider recognises key; otherwise null to pass resolution to the next provider in the chain.

api/Invex.FileSystem.IRootedFileSystem.h.title: Interface IRootedFileSystem | Invex.FileSystem
api/Invex.FileSystem.IRootedFileSystem.h.summary: Interface IRootedFileSystem Namespace Invex.FileSystem Assembly Invex.FileSystem.dll Defines a specialised file system abstraction, layering build-aware path resolution on top of the standard System.IO.Abstractions.IFileSystem abstraction. [PublicAPI] public interface IRootedFileSystem : IFileSystem Inherited Members IFileSystem.Directory IFileSystem.DirectoryInfo IFileSystem.DriveInfo IFileSystem.File IFileSystem.FileInfo IFileSystem.FileStream IFileSystem.FileSystemWatcher IFileSystem.FileVersionInfo IFileSystem.Path Remarks Path resolution is intentionally separate from file I/O: GetPath(string) queries a prioritized chain of IPathProvider implementations and caches the result. Callers never need to hard-code paths or know which provider resolved a key. Properties CurrentDirectory Gets the current working directory of the application as a RootedPath. RootedPath CurrentDirectory { get; } Property Value RootedPath Remarks Evaluated eagerly each time the property is read — it is not cached because the working directory can change during the lifetime of the process. FileSystem Gets the underlying System.IO.Abstractions.IFileSystem instance used for all file I/O operations. IFileSystem FileSystem { get; } Property Value IFileSystem Remarks All System.IO.Abstractions.IFileSystem members on this interface delegate to this property so that consumers of IRootedFileSystem work against a single, consistent abstraction regardless of which concrete file-system backend is registered. Methods CreateRootedPath(string) Creates a new RootedPath bound to this file system instance from a raw string path. RootedPath CreateRootedPath(string path) Parameters path string The absolute string path. Returns RootedPath A new RootedPath associated with this file system instance. Remarks Prefer this factory method over constructing RootedPath directly so that the resulting path is automatically bound to this IRootedFileSystem rather than to the inner FileSystem. GetPath(string) Resolves a well-known path by key, querying the registered IPathProvider chain and caching the result for later calls. RootedPath GetPath(string key) Parameters key string An application-defined key that identifies the path (e.g. "Root", "Artifacts"). Keys are compared by the individual providers and are conventionally defined as constants. Returns RootedPath A RootedPath corresponding to the key. Remarks Providers are queried in order until one returns a non-null result; providers that do not recognise the key are skipped over. When two providers can both resolve the same key, the one with the higher priority wins. Results are cached after the first successful resolution, so repeated calls with the same key always return the same RootedPath instance without re-querying providers. Exceptions InvalidOperationException Thrown when no registered provider can resolve key, or when the resolution depth exceeds the circular-dependency guard threshold. GetPath<T>() Resolves the path for a given IPathMarker type by invoking its static Path(IFileSystem) method directly. RootedPath GetPath<T>() where T : IPathMarker Returns RootedPath A RootedPath for the specified marker type. Type Parameters T A type that implements IPathMarker. Remarks Unlike GetPath(string), this overload bypasses the provider chain and the cache entirely — it calls T.Path(this) on every invocation. Use it for paths that are statically determined by the marker type itself, such as source-generated project paths.

api/Invex.FileSystem.RootedPath.html.title: Class RootedPath | Invex.FileSystem
api/Invex.FileSystem.RootedPath.html.summary: Class RootedPath Namespace Invex.FileSystem Assembly Invex.FileSystem.dll Represents a file system path that is rooted within a specific System.IO.Abstractions.IFileSystem instance. [PublicAPI] public record RootedPath : IEquatable<RootedPath> Inheritance object RootedPath Implements IEquatable<RootedPath> Inherited Members object.Equals(object) object.Equals(object, object) object.GetHashCode() object.GetType() object.MemberwiseClone() object.ReferenceEquals(object, object) Remarks Properties such as FileExists, FileName, and DirectoryName intentionally return null when the path does not match the expected entry type (file vs. directory), rather than throwing, so callers can use null-coalescing patterns naturally. Constructors RootedPath(IFileSystem, string) Represents a file system path that is rooted within a specific System.IO.Abstractions.IFileSystem instance. public RootedPath(IFileSystem FileSystem, string Path) Parameters FileSystem IFileSystem The file system instance this path belongs to. Path string The absolute path string. Remarks Properties such as FileExists, FileName, and DirectoryName intentionally return null when the path does not match the expected entry type (file vs. directory), rather than throwing, so callers can use null-coalescing patterns naturally. Properties DirectoryExists Indicates whether the path exists in the file system as a directory. public bool DirectoryExists { get; } Property Value bool DirectoryName Gets the path of the directory containing the current path, as computed by GetDirectoryName(string). public string? DirectoryName { get; } Property Value string The containing directory's path if the current path resolves to an existing directory; otherwise null. Returns null (rather than throwing) when the path points to a file or does not exist. Remarks GetDirectoryName(string) strips the last path segment and any trailing separator — it returns the parent path, not the directory's own name. For the last segment of the path itself, use FileNameWithoutExtension. FileExists Indicates whether the path exists in the file system as a file. public bool FileExists { get; } Property Value bool FileName Gets the file name (including extension) from the current path. public string? FileName { get; } Property Value string The file name if the path resolves to an existing file; otherwise null. Returns null (rather than throwing) when the path points to a directory or does not exist, so callers can safely use null-coalescing without a prior existence check. FileNameWithoutExtension Gets the file name of the current path without its extension. public string FileNameWithoutExtension { get; } Property Value string Remarks Unlike FileName, this property does not check whether the file exists — it is a pure path-string operation and always returns a value. FileSystem The file system instance this path belongs to. public IFileSystem FileSystem { get; init; } Property Value IFileSystem Parent Gets the parent directory of the current path. public RootedPath? Parent { get; } Property Value RootedPath A new RootedPath representing the parent directory, or null if the current path is already a root. Remarks A trailing directory separator on the input path is stripped before the parent is computed so that /foo/ and /foo both yield the same parent (/). The returned path always ends with System.IO.Abstractions.IPath.DirectorySeparatorChar so it can be combined further with the / operator without producing double separators. Path The absolute path string. public string Path { get; init; } Property Value string PathExists Indicates whether the path exists in the file system as either a file or a directory. public bool PathExists { get; } Property Value bool Remarks Equivalent to FileExists || DirectoryExists. Methods ToString() Returns a string that represents the current object. public override string ToString() Returns string A string that represents the current object. Operators operator /(RootedPath, string) Combines the current RootedPath with a relative string segment using Combine(string, string). public static RootedPath operator /(RootedPath left, string right) Parameters left RootedPath The base RootedPath. right string The relative segment to append. Returns RootedPath A new RootedPath representing the combined path. Remarks Using an operator instead of a method keeps path-construction code concise and readable, e.g. rootedFileSystem.GetPath("Root") / "src" / "MyProject". The operator preserves the original FileSystem reference so the resulting path remains bound to the same file-system abstraction. implicit operator string(RootedPath) Implicitly converts a RootedPath to its string representation. public static implicit operator string(RootedPath path) Parameters path RootedPath The RootedPath to convert. Returns string The string representation of the path. Remarks Allows a RootedPath to be passed directly to any API that accepts a string path without an explicit cast or .ToString() call.

api/Invex.FileSystem.TransformFileScope..title: Class TransformFileScope | Invex.FileSystem
api/Invex.FileSystem.TransformFileScope..summary: Class TransformFileScope Namespace Invex.FileSystem Assembly Invex.FileSystem.dll Provides disposable scopes for performing temporary, reversible transformations on a single file's content. [PublicAPI] public sealed class TransformFileScope : IAsyncDisposable, IDisposable Inheritance object TransformFileScope Implements IAsyncDisposable IDisposable Inherited Members object.Equals(object) object.Equals(object, object) object.GetHashCode() object.GetType() object.ReferenceEquals(object, object) object.ToString() Examples await using var scope = await TransformFileScope.CreateAsync( myProjectFile, content => content.Replace("1.0.0", "2.0.0")); // File now contains "2.0.0". Original is restored when scope is disposed. Remarks On creation the file's current content is captured. The supplied transform is applied and written back immediately. On disposal the original content is restored, or — if the file did not exist before the scope was opened — the file is deleted. This is useful during an operation when a file (e.g. a project file or a config file) needs to be temporarily modified for a specific step without permanently altering it. Call CancelRestore() before disposing to commit the transformation instead of rolling it back. Methods Add(Func<string, string>) Applies an additional transformation to the file within an already-open scope, using synchronous I/O. public TransformFileScope Add(Func<string, string> transform) Parameters transform Func<string, string> A function that receives the file's current (already-transformed) content and returns the next content to write. Returns TransformFileScope The same TransformFileScope instance, for fluent chaining. Remarks The scope still restores to the original content (captured at creation time), not the content at the point of the last Add call. Multiple calls build up transformations; disposal always unwinds them all at once. If CancelRestore() has been called, the transformation is skipped and the scope is returned unchanged. Exceptions ObjectDisposedException Thrown if the scope has already been disposed. AddAsync(Func<string, string>, CancellationToken) Applies an additional transformation to the file within an already-open scope, using asynchronous I/O. public Task<TransformFileScope> AddAsync(Func<string, string> transform, CancellationToken cancellationToken = default) Parameters transform Func<string, string> A function that receives the file's current (already-transformed) content and returns the next content to write. cancellationToken CancellationToken Token to observe for cancellation. If cancelled during the write, the file is restored to its state at the time the scope was created before the exception propagates. Returns Task<TransformFileScope> The same TransformFileScope instance, for fluent chaining. Remarks The scope still restores to the original content (captured at creation time), not the content at the point of the last Add call. Multiple calls build up transformations; disposal always unwinds them all at once. If CancelRestore() has been called, the transformation is skipped and the scope is returned unchanged. Exceptions ObjectDisposedException Thrown if the scope has already been disposed. OperationCanceledException Thrown (and the file restored) if cancellationToken is cancelled during the write. CancelRestore() Prevents the file from being restored to its original content when the scope is disposed. public void CancelRestore() Remarks Call this when the transformation should be made permanent — for example after a build step has successfully consumed the transformed file and the change no longer needs to be rolled back. Once called it cannot be undone. Create(RootedPath, Func<string, string>) Creates a new TransformFileScope, applying a synchronous transformation to the target file. public static TransformFileScope Create(RootedPath file, Func<string, string> transform) Parameters file RootedPath The file to transform. transform Func<string, string> A function that receives the file's current content (or an empty string if the file does not yet exist) and returns the new content to write. Returns TransformFileScope A TransformFileScope that will restore the file when disposed. Remarks If the file does not exist it is created on disk, the transform receives an empty string, and disposing the scope deletes the file rather than restoring content. CreateAsync(RootedPath, Func<string, string>, CancellationToken) Creates a new TransformFileScope, applying an asynchronous transformation to the target file. public static Task<TransformFileScope> CreateAsync(RootedPath file, Func<string, string> transform, CancellationToken cancellationToken = default) Parameters file RootedPath The file to transform. transform Func<string, string> A function that receives the file's current content (or an empty string if the file does not yet exist) and returns the new content to write. cancellationToken CancellationToken Token to observe for cancellation. If cancelled while the transformed content is being written, the file is restored to its state at the time the scope was created before the exception propagates. Returns Task<TransformFileScope> A TransformFileScope that will restore the file when disposed. Remarks If the file does not exist it is created on disk, the transform receives an empty string, and disposing the scope deletes the file rather than restoring content. Exceptions OperationCanceledException Thrown (and the file restored) if cancellationToken is cancelled while the transformed content is being written. Dispose() Synchronously disposes the scope and restores the file to its original state. public void Dispose() Remarks If CancelRestore() was called, the file is left as-is. If the file did not exist when the scope was created, it is deleted. Otherwise the original content is written back. Calling this method more than once is safe — subsequent calls are no-ops. Prefer DisposeAsync() (via await using) where possible so the restore write does not block the calling thread. DisposeAsync() Asynchronously disposes the scope and restores the file to its original state. public ValueTask DisposeAsync() Returns ValueTask Remarks If CancelRestore() was called, the file is left as-is. If the file did not exist when the scope was created, it is deleted. Otherwise the original content is written back. Calling this method more than once is safe — subsequent calls are no-ops. No cancellation token is accepted here: we prefer to wait for the write to complete so the file is not left in a partially-written state.

api/Invex.FileSystem.TransformFileScopeE.title: Class TransformFileScopeExtensions | Invex.FileSystem
api/Invex.FileSystem.TransformFileScopeE.summary: Class TransformFileScopeExtensions Namespace Invex.FileSystem Assembly Invex.FileSystem.dll Provides extension methods for fluently chaining additional transformations onto a Task<TResult> that yields a scope returned by one of the static CreateAsync factory methods. [PublicAPI] public static class TransformFileScopeExtensions Inheritance object TransformFileScopeExtensions Inherited Members object.Equals(object) object.Equals(object, object) object.GetHashCode() object.GetType() object.MemberwiseClone() object.ReferenceEquals(object, object) object.ToString() Remarks These extensions exist so that CreateAsync and one or more AddAsync calls can be written as a single expression without intermediate variables: await using var scope = await TransformFileScope .CreateAsync(file, _ => "v1") .AddAsync(c => c + "-patched"); Without them, the caller would need to await CreateAsync into a local and then await AddAsync separately. Methods AddAsync(Task<TransformFileScope>, Func<string, string>) Applies an additional asynchronous transformation to the file within the TransformFileScope returned by scopeTask. public static Task<TransformFileScope> AddAsync(this Task<TransformFileScope> scopeTask, Func<string, string> transform) Parameters scopeTask Task<TransformFileScope> A task that produces a TransformFileScope. transform Func<string, string> A function to apply to the file's current content. Returns Task<TransformFileScope> A task that completes with the same TransformFileScope instance after the transformation has been applied. Remarks This overload does not accept a CancellationToken; the inner AddAsync(Func<string, string>, CancellationToken) call runs with None. Call that method directly if cancellation support is required. AddAsync(Task<TransformMultiFileScope>, Func<string, string>) Applies an additional asynchronous transformation to each file within the TransformMultiFileScope returned by scopeTask. public static Task<TransformMultiFileScope> AddAsync(this Task<TransformMultiFileScope> scopeTask, Func<string, string> transform) Parameters scopeTask Task<TransformMultiFileScope> A task that produces a TransformMultiFileScope. transform Func<string, string> A function to apply to each file's current content. Returns Task<TransformMultiFileScope> A task that completes with the same TransformMultiFileScope instance after the transformation has been applied. Remarks This overload does not accept a CancellationToken; the inner AddAsync(Func<string, string>, CancellationToken) call runs with None. Call that method directly if cancellation support is required.

api/Invex.FileSystem.TransformMultiFileS.title: Class TransformMultiFileScope | Invex.FileSystem
api/Invex.FileSystem.TransformMultiFileS.summary: Class TransformMultiFileScope Namespace Invex.FileSystem Assembly Invex.FileSystem.dll Provides disposable scopes for performing temporary, reversible transformations on multiple files simultaneously. Upon disposal, the original content of every managed file is restored unless restoration is explicitly cancelled. [PublicAPI] public sealed class TransformMultiFileScope : IAsyncDisposable, IDisposable Inheritance object TransformMultiFileScope Implements IAsyncDisposable IDisposable Inherited Members object.Equals(object) object.Equals(object, object) object.GetHashCode() object.GetType() object.ReferenceEquals(object, object) object.ToString() Remarks The behaviour mirrors TransformFileScope but operates on a collection of files in parallel (via Task.WhenAll). Each file's original content is captured independently: files that did not exist before the scope was created are deleted on disposal, while existing files have their content written back. This is useful when a build step needs to simultaneously patch a set of related files — for example injecting a shared version number into multiple project files — and must guarantee that all files are restored even if the step fails partway through. Call CancelRestore() before disposing to commit all transformations permanently instead of rolling them back. Methods Add(Func<string, string>) Applies an additional synchronous transformation to every file within an already-open scope. public TransformMultiFileScope Add(Func<string, string> transform) Parameters transform Func<string, string> A function that receives each file's current (already-transformed) content and returns the next content to write. Returns TransformMultiFileScope The same TransformMultiFileScope instance, for fluent chaining. Remarks The scope always restores to the original contents captured at creation time, not the state after the most recent Add call. If CancelRestore() has been called, the transformation is skipped and the scope is returned unchanged. Exceptions ObjectDisposedException Thrown if the scope has already been disposed. AddAsync(Func<string, string>, CancellationToken) Applies an additional asynchronous transformation to every file within an already-open scope. public Task<TransformMultiFileScope> AddAsync(Func<string, string> transform, CancellationToken cancellationToken = default) Parameters transform Func<string, string> A function that receives each file's current (already-transformed) content and returns the next content to write. cancellationToken CancellationToken Token to observe for cancellation. If cancelled during the write, all files are restored to their state at the time the scope was created before the exception propagates. Returns Task<TransformMultiFileScope> The same TransformMultiFileScope instance, for fluent chaining. Remarks The scope always restores to the original contents captured at creation time, not the state after the most recent Add call. If CancelRestore() has been called, the transformation is skipped and the scope is returned unchanged. Exceptions ObjectDisposedException Thrown if the scope has already been disposed. OperationCanceledException Thrown (and all files restored) if cancellationToken is cancelled during the write. CancelRestore() Prevents all files from being restored to their original content when the scope is disposed. public void CancelRestore() Remarks Call this when the transformation should be made permanent — for example after a build step has successfully consumed the transformed file and the change no longer needs to be rolled back. Once called it cannot be undone. Create(IEnumerable<RootedPath>, Func<string, string>) Creates a new TransformMultiFileScope, applying a synchronous transformation to each file in files. public static TransformMultiFileScope Create(IEnumerable<RootedPath> files, Func<string, string> transform) Parameters files IEnumerable<RootedPath> The files to transform. transform Func<string, string> A function applied to each file's current content (or an empty string for files that do not yet exist). Returns TransformMultiFileScope A TransformMultiFileScope that will restore all files when disposed. Remarks files is enumerated exactly once; files are read and written sequentially. Use CreateAsync(IEnumerable<RootedPath>, Func<string, string>, CancellationToken) to process files in parallel. Files that do not exist are created on disk, the transform receives an empty string for them, and disposing the scope deletes them rather than restoring content. CreateAsync(IEnumerable<RootedPath>, Func<string, string>, CancellationToken) Creates a new TransformMultiFileScope, applying an asynchronous transformation to each file in files. public static Task<TransformMultiFileScope> CreateAsync(IEnumerable<RootedPath> files, Func<string, string> transform, CancellationToken cancellationToken = default) Parameters files IEnumerable<RootedPath> The files to transform. transform Func<string, string> A function applied to each file's current content (or an empty string for files that do not yet exist). The same function is called for every file; if you need per-file logic consider multiple TransformFileScope instances instead. cancellationToken CancellationToken Token to observe for cancellation. If cancelled while the transformed content is being written, all files are restored to their state at the time the scope was created before the exception propagates. Returns Task<TransformMultiFileScope> A TransformMultiFileScope that will restore all files when disposed. Remarks files is enumerated exactly once; files are read and written in parallel via WhenAll(IEnumerable<Task>). Files that do not exist are created on disk, the transform receives an empty string for them, and disposing the scope deletes them rather than restoring content. Exceptions OperationCanceledException Thrown (and all files restored) if cancellationToken is cancelled while the transformed content is being written. Dispose() Synchronously disposes the scope and restores all managed files to their original state. public void Dispose() Remarks If CancelRestore() was called, all files are left as-is. Files that did not exist when the scope was created are deleted. All other files have their original content written back, one at a time. Calling this method more than once is safe — subsequent calls are no-ops. Prefer DisposeAsync() (via await using) where possible: it restores files in parallel and does not block the calling thread. DisposeAsync() Asynchronously disposes the scope and restores all managed files to their original state. public ValueTask DisposeAsync() Returns ValueTask Remarks If CancelRestore() was called, all files are left as-is. Files that did not exist when the scope was created are deleted. All other files have their original content written back in parallel. Calling this method more than once is safe — subsequent calls are no-ops. No cancellation token is accepted: restoring files should always complete to avoid leaving the repository in a partially-modified state.

api/Invex.FileSystem.html.title: Namespace Invex.FileSystem | Invex.FileSystem
api/Invex.FileSystem.html.summary: Namespace Invex.FileSystem Classes FileSystemHostExtensions Provides extension methods for registering the file system services with an IServiceCollection. FunctionPathProvider A lightweight, delegate-based implementation of IPathProvider that resolves paths using a caller-supplied function rather than requiring a dedicated class. RootedPath Represents a file system path that is rooted within a specific System.IO.Abstractions.IFileSystem instance. TransformFileScope Provides disposable scopes for performing temporary, reversible transformations on a single file's content. TransformFileScopeExtensions Provides extension methods for fluently chaining additional transformations onto a Task<TResult> that yields a scope returned by one of the static CreateAsync factory methods. TransformMultiFileScope Provides disposable scopes for performing temporary, reversible transformations on multiple files simultaneously. Upon disposal, the original content of every managed file is restored unless restoration is explicitly cancelled. Interfaces IPathMarker Defines a contract for types that represent a specific, well-known file path within the file system, resolved relative to the current System.IO.Abstractions.IFileSystem instance. IPathProvider Defines a provider that can resolve a RootedPath for a given key within the file system. IRootedFileSystem Defines a specialised file system abstraction, layering build-aware path resolution on top of the standard System.IO.Abstractions.IFileSystem abstraction.

api/index.html.title: API Reference | Invex.FileSystem
api/index.html.summary: API Reference This section contains the generated API reference for every public type in the Invex.FileSystem namespace. Key types Type Description IRootedFileSystem File system abstraction with GetPath, CurrentDirectory, and CreateRootedPath. RootedPath A path bound to an IFileSystem, with composition and existence helpers. IPathProvider Resolves a RootedPath for a key; queried by priority. FunctionPathProvider Delegate-based IPathProvider implementation. IPathMarker Strongly-typed, statically-computed path. TransformFileScope Temporary, reversible single-file transformation. TransformMultiFileScope Temporary, reversible multi-file transformation. TransformFileScopeExtensions Fluent AddAsync chaining for transform scope tasks. FileSystemHostExtensions DI registration: AddRootedFileSystem and ProvidePath. For conceptual documentation and usage examples, see the Docs section.

docs/getting-started.html.title: Getting Started | Invex.FileSystem
docs/getting-started.html.summary: Getting Started Installation dotnet add package Invex.FileSystem The package targets .NET 8.0, .NET 9.0, and .NET 10.0. Registering with dependency injection Invex.FileSystem is designed to be wired up through Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection. A single call registers everything: using Invex.FileSystem; using Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection; var services = new ServiceCollection(); services.AddLogging(); // IRootedFileSystem logs path resolutions at Debug level services.AddRootedFileSystem(); AddRootedFileSystem() registers: Service Lifetime Notes IFileSystem (keyed: "RootedFileSystem") Singleton The real System.IO.Abstractions.FileSystem backing implementation. IRootedFileSystem Singleton The rooted wrapper, with all registered IPathProviders sorted by descending priority. IFileSystem (non-keyed) Singleton Resolves to the same instance as IRootedFileSystem, so existing code that depends on IFileSystem automatically gets the rooted implementation. Declaring well-known paths After AddRootedFileSystem(), declare where your well-known locations live with ProvidePath: services.ProvidePath((key, fs) => key switch { "Root" => fs.CurrentDirectory, "Artifacts" => fs.GetPath("Root") / "artifacts", "Logs" => fs.GetPath("Artifacts") / "logs", _ => null, // defer to the next provider }); Returning null for unrecognized keys is important — it lets other providers handle those keys. See Path Providers for priorities, ordering, and custom implementations. Resolving and using paths using var provider = services.BuildServiceProvider(); var fs = provider.GetRequiredService<IRootedFileSystem>(); // Resolve well-known locations by key. RootedPath artifacts = fs.GetPath("Artifacts"); // Compose further with the / operator. RootedPath logFile = artifacts / "logs" / "build.log"; // Query and perform I/O — RootedPath implicitly converts to string. if (!logFile.PathExists) fs.Directory.CreateDirectory(logFile.Parent!); fs.File.WriteAllText(logFile, "hello"); Key things to notice: GetPath results are cached — fs.GetPath("Root") always returns the same instance after the first call. RootedPath implicitly converts to string, so it can be passed to any API expecting a path. All I/O members (File, Directory, Path, …) come from IFileSystem and delegate to the inner file system. Without dependency injection DI is convenient but not required. You can construct a RootedPath directly over any IFileSystem: using System.IO.Abstractions; var fileSystem = new FileSystem(); var path = new RootedPath(fileSystem, @"C:\repos\my-project"); RootedPath csproj = path / "src" / "App" / "App.csproj"; bool exists = csproj.FileExists; Next steps Rooted Paths — everything RootedPath can do. Path Providers — priorities, chaining, and custom providers. File Transform Scopes — temporary, reversible file edits.

docs/introduction.html.title: Introduction | Invex.FileSystem
docs/introduction.html.summary: Introduction Invex.FileSystem is a strongly-typed file system library for .NET. It builds on System.IO.Abstractions and adds two core capabilities: Keyed path resolution — resolve well-known locations (such as "Root" or "Artifacts") by key through a prioritized, cached chain of providers, instead of hard-coding paths or scattering magic strings. Reversible file transformations — apply temporary edits to one or many files inside a disposable scope, with the original content restored automatically when the scope is disposed. Why? Build scripts, code generators, and developer tooling all share the same pain points: Paths are stringly-typed. A typo in "artifcats/logs" compiles fine and fails at runtime. Paths are computed everywhere. Each call site re-derives "the repo root" or "the output folder" in its own way, and they drift apart over time. Temporary file edits leak. A step that patches a version number into a project file and then crashes leaves the repository dirty. File I/O is hard to test. Code that calls System.IO.File directly needs a real disk to test against. Invex.FileSystem addresses each of these: Pain point Solution Stringly-typed paths RootedPath composes with the / operator and carries its own IFileSystem. Duplicated path logic IPathProvider resolves each key once, in one place, with caching. Leaky temporary edits TransformFileScope restores original content on dispose, guaranteed. Untestable I/O Everything works against IFileSystem, so a MockFileSystem drops straight in (Testing). The pieces at a glance ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ IRootedFileSystem (implements IFileSystem) │ │ │ │ GetPath("Root") ──► IPathProvider chain │ │ GetPath<TMarker>() ──► static marker resolution │ │ CurrentDirectory ──► RootedPath │ │ CreateRootedPath(...) ──► RootedPath │ │ │ │ File / Directory / Path / ... ──► inner IFileSystem │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ IRootedFileSystem is a drop-in IFileSystem with path resolution layered on top. Code that only needs file I/O can keep depending on IFileSystem; code that needs well-known locations depends on IRootedFileSystem. RootedPath is a record pairing an absolute path string with the IFileSystem it belongs to, so existence checks and composition need no extra context. IPathProvider implementations answer "where is X?" — one provider per concern, queried in priority order. TransformFileScope / TransformMultiFileScope make temporary file edits safe. Next steps Head to Getting Started to install the package and wire it into your dependency-injection container.

docs/path-markers.html.title: Path Markers | Invex.FileSystem
docs/path-markers.html.summary: Path Markers IPathMarker gives a compile-time name to a path that is statically determined by a type, rather than resolved through the provider chain. public interface IPathMarker { static abstract RootedPath Path(IFileSystem fileSystem); } When to use markers vs. keys Aspect Keyed lookup (GetPath("X")) Marker lookup (GetPath<TMarker>()) Identified by A string key A type Resolved by The registered IPathProvider chain The marker type's own static Path method Cached? Yes — once per key No — computed on every call Typo safety Runtime failure Compile-time failure Best for Locations configured per-application Locations intrinsic to a type (e.g. source-generated project paths) Defining a marker public sealed class ArtifactsPath : IPathMarker { public static RootedPath Path(IFileSystem fileSystem) => ((IRootedFileSystem)fileSystem).GetPath("Root") / "artifacts"; } Resolving a marker RootedPath artifacts = fs.GetPath<ArtifactsPath>(); GetPath<T>() simply invokes T.Path(this) — it bypasses the provider chain and the cache entirely. Because the current IRootedFileSystem is passed in, the returned RootedPath stays consistent with every other path resolved by the application (and with mock file systems in tests). Note Markers use C#'s static abstract interface members, so they require .NET 8+ and cannot be instantiated — the marker type exists purely to carry the path computation. Combining markers with providers Markers and providers compose naturally — a marker can build on keyed lookups, as in the example above, and a provider can return a marker-derived path: services.ProvidePath((key, fs) => key == "PackageOutput" ? fs.GetPath<ArtifactsPath>() / "packages" : null);

docs/path-providers.html.title: Path Providers | Invex.FileSystem
docs/path-providers.html.summary: Path Providers Path resolution is decoupled from file I/O. IRootedFileSystem.GetPath(string) queries every registered IPathProvider in descending priority order and returns the first non-null result, caching it for subsequent calls. GetPath("Artifacts") │ ├─► Provider (priority 5) → null (doesn't recognise the key — skipped) ├─► Provider (priority 1) → RootedPath (first non-null result wins) └─► Provider (priority 0) (never reached for this key) Registering providers inline The ProvidePath extension methods register a delegate-backed provider (FunctionPathProvider) without needing a dedicated class: // Simple key -> path mapping (priority defaults to 1). services.ProvidePath(key => key == "Temp" ? new RootedPath(new FileSystem(), Path.GetTempPath()) : null); // Provider that depends on another resolved path. services.ProvidePath((key, fs) => key == "Output" ? fs.GetPath("Root") / "output" : null, priority: 5); Important Use the (key, fs) overload whenever the path depends on another resolved path. The IRootedFileSystem is resolved lazily when the provider is first invoked, which avoids circular-construction problems at startup. Capturing the raw IServiceProvider in a closure and resolving IRootedFileSystem yourself risks a circular dependency. Priorities and ordering Higher values are queried first. When two providers can both resolve the same key, the one with the higher priority wins. Equal priorities are queried in registration order (the sort is stable). Use a negative priority for a low-priority fallback that only applies when no other provider matches. A provider that does not recognize a key must return null to pass resolution to the next provider in the chain. Caching Results are cached per key after the first successful resolution. Repeated calls to GetPath("Root") return the same RootedPath instance without re-querying providers — so each provider's GetPath is called at most once per key per IRootedFileSystem lifetime. Custom provider implementations For complex resolution logic — searching the file system, reading configuration, combining multiple keys — implement IPathProvider directly and register it as a service: public sealed class GitRootPathProvider(IFileSystem fileSystem) : IPathProvider { public int Priority => 10; public RootedPath? GetPath(string key) { if (key != "GitRoot") return null; var dir = new RootedPath(fileSystem, fileSystem.Directory.GetCurrentDirectory()); while (dir is not null && !(dir / ".git").DirectoryExists) dir = dir.Parent; return dir; } } services.AddSingleton<IPathProvider, GitRootPathProvider>(); services.AddRootedFileSystem(); Note Registration order relative to AddRootedFileSystem() does not matter — providers are collected from the container when IRootedFileSystem is first resolved. Error handling If no provider recognizes a key, GetPath throws InvalidOperationException: Could not locate path for key 'Artifcats' Providers may call GetPath for other keys while resolving their own (as in the "Output" example above). A recursion-depth guard throws InvalidOperationException if resolution nests more than 100 levels deep, which almost always indicates a circular key dependency such as "A" → "B" → "A".

docs/rooted-paths.html.title: Rooted Paths | Invex.FileSystem
docs/rooted-paths.html.summary: Rooted Paths RootedPath is the core value type of the library: a record that binds an absolute path string to the IFileSystem it belongs to. Because the file system travels with the path, existence checks, queries, and composition need no extra context. public record RootedPath(IFileSystem FileSystem, string Path) Creating a rooted path // From the rooted file system (preferred — binds to IRootedFileSystem): RootedPath root = fs.GetPath("Root"); RootedPath current = fs.CurrentDirectory; RootedPath custom = fs.CreateRootedPath(@"C:\data"); // Directly, over any IFileSystem: var path = new RootedPath(new FileSystem(), @"C:\data"); Tip When working with an IRootedFileSystem, prefer CreateRootedPath over the constructor so the resulting path is bound to the rooted wrapper rather than the inner file system. Composing paths Use the / operator to append segments. Each application returns a new RootedPath bound to the same file system: RootedPath projectFile = fs.GetPath("Root") / "src" / "App" / "App.csproj"; The operator delegates to IPath.Combine, so platform-appropriate separators are used automatically. Querying paths Member Returns Behaviour PathExists bool true if the path exists as a file or a directory. FileExists bool true if the path exists as a file. DirectoryExists bool true if the path exists as a directory. FileName string? File name with extension; null if the path is not an existing file. FileNameWithoutExtension string Pure string operation — always returns a value, no existence check. DirectoryName string? Containing directory's path; null if the path is not an existing directory. Parent RootedPath? The parent directory; null when the path is already a root. Note Members that depend on the entry type (FileName, DirectoryName) intentionally return null instead of throwing when the path doesn't match, so you can use null-coalescing patterns naturally: string name = path.FileName ?? "(not a file)"; Parent Parent strips any trailing separator before computing the parent, so /foo/ and /foo both yield /. The returned path always ends with a directory separator, so it can be combined further with / without producing double separators: RootedPath? parent = (fs.CreateRootedPath(@"C:\a\b\c")).Parent; // C:\a\b\ RootedPath sibling = parent! / "d"; // C:\a\b\d Using paths with standard APIs RootedPath implicitly converts to string, and ToString() returns the raw path, so it can be handed to anything that takes a path: RootedPath path = fs.GetPath("Root") / "notes.txt"; File.ReadAllText(path); // implicit string conversion Console.WriteLine($"Path: {path}"); // ToString() Value semantics As a record, RootedPath has structural equality — two instances are equal when they have the same FileSystem reference and Path string. Use with expressions to derive new paths: RootedPath renamed = path with { Path = path.Path.Replace(".txt", ".md") };

docs/testing.html.title: Testing | Invex.FileSystem
docs/testing.html.summary: Testing Because the library builds on System.IO.Abstractions, everything can be exercised against an in-memory MockFileSystem — no real disk required. dotnet add package TestableIO.System.IO.Abstractions.TestingHelpers Testing with RootedPath directly Construct a RootedPath over a MockFileSystem and use it exactly like a real path: using System.IO.Abstractions.TestingHelpers; var mock = new MockFileSystem(); var path = new RootedPath(mock, "/repo/file.txt"); mock.AddFile(path, new MockFileData("hello")); Assert.True(path.FileExists); Assert.Equal("file.txt", path.FileName); Testing code that depends on IRootedFileSystem Wire up the DI container with a mock file system by overriding the keyed backing registration: var mock = new MockFileSystem(new Dictionary<string, MockFileData> { ["/repo/src/App/App.csproj"] = new("<Project />"), }, "/repo"); var services = new ServiceCollection(); services.AddLogging(); services.AddRootedFileSystem(); // Replace the real file system with the mock. services.AddKeyedSingleton<IFileSystem>("RootedFileSystem", mock); services.ProvidePath((key, fs) => key switch { "Root" => fs.CurrentDirectory, _ => null, }); using var provider = services.BuildServiceProvider(); var fs = provider.GetRequiredService<IRootedFileSystem>(); RootedPath csproj = fs.GetPath("Root") / "src" / "App" / "App.csproj"; Assert.True(csproj.FileExists); Note MockFileSystem lets you set the current directory (the second constructor argument above), which makes IRootedFileSystem.CurrentDirectory fully deterministic in tests. Testing transform scopes Transform scopes work over whatever file system the RootedPath carries, so rollback behaviour is easy to verify: var mock = new MockFileSystem(); var file = new RootedPath(mock, "/config.json"); mock.AddFile(file, new MockFileData("""{ "version": "1.0.0" }""")); await using (await TransformFileScope.CreateAsync(file, c => c.Replace("1.0.0", "2.0.0"))) { Assert.Contains("2.0.0", mock.File.ReadAllText(file)); } // Restored after dispose. Assert.Contains("1.0.0", mock.File.ReadAllText(file)); Faking path resolution in unit tests For code that takes IRootedFileSystem, you rarely need a full container — a provider registered inline is often enough, or you can substitute the interface entirely with your mocking library of choice (the interface's I/O members have default implementations that delegate to the FileSystem property, so only FileSystem and GetPath need stubbing).

docs/transform-scopes.html.title: File Transform Scopes | Invex.FileSystem
docs/transform-scopes.html.summary: File Transform Scopes TransformFileScope and TransformMultiFileScope apply temporary, reversible edits to files. On creation the original content is captured and the transform is written immediately; on disposal the original content is restored — unless you commit the change with CancelRestore(). This is ideal for operations that must temporarily patch a file — injecting a version number into a project file, toggling a feature flag in a config file — and must guarantee the file is returned to its original state even when the operation fails partway through. Single file RootedPath projectFile = fs.GetPath("Root") / "Directory.Build.props"; await using (var scope = await TransformFileScope.CreateAsync( projectFile, content => content.Replace("1.0.0", "2.0.0"))) { // The file now contains "2.0.0" — run your build step here. } // On dispose the original content is restored automatically. Synchronous variants exist for non-async contexts: using var scope = TransformFileScope.Create(projectFile, c => c.Replace("1.0.0", "2.0.0")); Multiple files TransformMultiFileScope applies the same transform to a set of files, processing them in parallel (async) and restoring all of them on dispose: RootedPath[] projects = [ fs.GetPath("Root") / "src" / "A" / "A.csproj", fs.GetPath("Root") / "src" / "B" / "B.csproj", ]; await using var scope = await TransformMultiFileScope.CreateAsync( projects, content => content.Replace("<Version>1.0.0</Version>", "<Version>2.0.0</Version>")); Chaining additional transforms Both scope types support Add / AddAsync to layer further transformations onto an open scope, and extension methods allow the whole pipeline to be written as a single fluent expression: await using var scope = await TransformFileScope .CreateAsync(projectFile, _ => "version=1") .AddAsync(content => content + "\nbuild=ci"); Note No matter how many Add calls are made, disposal restores the original content captured when the scope was created — the transformations are unwound all at once, not one by one. Tip The fluent Task<...>.AddAsync(...) extensions do not forward a CancellationToken. If you need cancellation support for the additional transform, await the scope into a local and call its AddAsync overload directly. Committing instead of rolling back Call CancelRestore() to keep the transformed content permanently: await using var scope = await TransformFileScope.CreateAsync(file, c => Patch(c)); RunBuildStep(); scope.CancelRestore(); // success — commit the change; dispose becomes a no-op Once CancelRestore() has been called it cannot be undone, and subsequent Add / AddAsync calls are skipped. Behaviour reference Situation Behaviour File does not exist at creation The file is created; the transform receives an empty string; disposal deletes the file. CancellationToken cancelled mid-write The file(s) are restored, then OperationCanceledException propagates. Add / AddAsync after dispose Throws ObjectDisposedException. Add / AddAsync after CancelRestore No-op; the scope is returned unchanged. Dispose called multiple times Safe — subsequent calls are no-ops. Sync Dispose on the multi-file scope Restores files sequentially; prefer await using for parallel restore. Important Disposal intentionally accepts no cancellation token: restoring content always runs to completion so a file is never left in a partially-written state.
